display_name: A shaper adds latency by buffering excess; a policer drops it — same rate limit, opposite symptom
context: Investigating an application complaint (latency or loss) on a link where a rate limit is applied via shaping or policing
observation: Traffic exceeding a configured rate is either delayed (rising latency/jitter) or dropped, depending on which mechanism is in use
naive_interpretation: Shaping and policing are interchangeable rate limits, so the choice does not affect the symptom
correct_interpretation: Both enforce a rate, but the failure mode differs. A shaper queues excess traffic and releases it later, so the symptom is added latency and jitter (and drops only once the shaping buffer overflows) — no loss until then. A policer makes an instantaneous conform/exceed decision and drops or remarks excess immediately, so the symptom is loss with no added delay. If the complaint is latency/jitter, suspect a shaper upstream; if it is loss on an otherwise idle path, suspect a policer.
why: A shaper holds conforming-exceeding packets in a queue and meters them out at the target rate, trading delay for delivery
A policer uses a token bucket to decide per-packet conform/exceed and acts immediately, trading delivery for zero added delay (RFC 2698 token-bucket model)
The same configured rate therefore presents as latency in one case and loss in the other — the mechanism, not the rate, determines the symptom
Shaper buffers are finite, so sustained over-rate eventually drops too, but only after the queue fills

confirmation_commands.purpose: Identify whether the class uses shaping (queue depth/latency) or policing (conform/exceed drops)
Distinguish shape counters and queue depth from police exceed/violate drops

traps: Hunting for packet loss when a shaper is the cause and the real symptom is latency/jitter with no drops yet
Assuming an idle link cannot add latency — a shaper delays bursts even when average utilization is low
Treating shaping and policing as the same tool when they produce opposite symptoms for the same rate
Ignoring shaper buffer overflow, which finally produces drops only after sustained over-rate

sources.title: RFC 2698 — A Two Rate Three Color Marker
